subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around you right now. The spoon you eat your bariis with, the pot your mother boils camel milk in, the corrugated iron roof over your head, the plastic jerrycan you carry water in, the glass in the window, the lemon in the kitchen and the bar of soap by the washing basin - every single one of these is made from a material, and every material was chosen because of its properties. A property is simply a feature of a material and how it behaves. In this unit you will become a materials detective. You will learn why cooking pots are made of metal but their handles are often wrapped in plastic or cloth, why the iron sheets on a coastal roof in Berbera or Mombasa go rusty and orange while a gold ring stays shiny for a lifetime, and why a squeeze of…
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Science 0893 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit you will be able to:
List and describe the main properties of metals and of non-metals, and give everyday examples of each.
Explain why a particular material is chosen for a particular job by linking its property to its use.
Define an alloy and use simple particle ideas to explain why an alloy is harder than the pure metal it comes from.
Separate a mixture by choosing the right method - filtering, evaporating or using a magnet - based on the properties of the substances inside it.
Recognise acids and alkalis, name common household examples, and state how to handle them safely.
Make a natural indicator and use it to test whether a household liquid is acidic, alkaline or neutral.
Read the pH scale and place a substance on it, and explain in simple terms what neutralisation means.
Identify common hazard warning symbols and say what each one tells you to do.
Study this table carefully. These words are the tools of a materials scientist. Notice how the example column ties each word to something you already know.

concepts.title: The Metals
concepts.explanation: Metals are among the most useful materials in the world. Think of everything made from metal in a busy market in Hargeisa or Nairobi: knives, pots, spoons, hoes, machetes, coins, wire, roofing sheets, water taps, padlocks and jewellery. Metals do so many different jobs because they share a set of very handy properties. Let us build up a picture of what makes a metal a metal. Metals do not crack or shatter easily. When you drop a metal spoon on the floor it bounces and stays whole; drop a clay cup and it smashes.…
concepts.example: When a metal is freshly cut or polished it reflects light and looks bright and shiny. This is why gold and silver are prized for jewellery - a polished gold ring catches the light beautifully, and because gold does not…

concepts.title: The Non-metals
concepts.explanation: The non-metals are the other great family. There are fewer of them than metals, but they are just as important. In many ways non-metals are the mirror-image of metals: where a metal is shiny, a non-metal is dull; where a metal bends, a non-metal snaps. Let us look at their properties. Dull, not shiny - non-metals do not reflect light well and often feel rough. Think of a lump of charcoal or a piece of sulfur.
concepts.example: Brittle - solid non-metals shatter when you drop or hit them. Charcoal crumbles; it is not malleable at all.

concepts.title: Metal Mixtures Called Alloys
concepts.explanation: Here is a clever secret that people discovered thousands of years ago: if you melt two metals together and let them cool, you get a new material that is often much better than either metal on its own. A mixture of metals like this is called an alloy. In an alloy the atoms of the different metals mix together, but they do not chemically bond - it is still a mixture, not a compound. The very first alloy people learned to make was bronze, a mixture of copper and tin. Bronze is harder than either copper or tin alone,…
concepts.example: The most important alloy in the modern world is steel. Steel is an unusual alloy because one of the ingredients is not a metal at all - it is the non-metal carbon. Steel is a mixture of iron and a small amount of…

concepts.title: Separating Mixtures
concepts.explanation: A mixture is made of different substances that are simply mixed together and not chemically joined. Because the substances keep their own properties, we can pull a mixture apart by cleverly using the differences between those properties. Choosing the right method is a real scientific skill. Using a magnet - if one substance is magnetic and the other is not, a magnet separates them. A mixture of iron filings and sulfur can be separated because iron is magnetic and sulfur is not.
concepts.example: Filtering - if you have a solid that will not dissolve mixed into a liquid, pour it through filter paper. The liquid passes through but the solid stays behind. This is how you would clear the sand out of sandy water -…

concepts.title: Acids and Alkalis
concepts.explanation: Some of the most interesting materials of all are the liquids we call acids and alkalis. You already know many of them, even if you did not know their scientific names. Foods that taste sour and sharp usually contain an acid. When you suck a slice of lemon or lime, that mouth-puckering sourness comes from citric acid - a weak acid. Vinegar, used in cooking and for cleaning, contains a weak acid too. Even the drink of cola contains acid. Your own stomach makes a much stronger acid to help break down food. Weak…
concepts.example: Alkalis are the chemical opposites of acids. Many cleaning products contain them. Soap and soapy water are mildly alkaline, which is why they feel slippery between your fingers. Baking soda (bicarbonate of soda)…

concepts.title: Indicators and the pH Scale
concepts.explanation: How can you tell whether a mystery liquid is an acid or an alkali without tasting it (which you must never do)? You use an indicator. An indicator is a special substance that turns one colour in an acid and a different colour in an alkali. It is like a colour-changing spy that reveals the secret nature of a liquid. The wonderful thing is that you do not need a laboratory to get an indicator. The bright colours of many plants work beautifully. Red cabbage juice is the most famous: it turns red or pink in an acid,…
concepts.example: A very common laboratory indicator is litmus, usually soaked into small strips of paper. Litmus follows a simple rule: it turns red in acids, blue in alkalis, and stays purple in a neutral substance such as pure water.…

explorations.title: The Metal and Non-metal Property Hunt
explorations.context: To investigate everyday objects and decide whether each is made of a metal or a non-metal by testing its properties.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down: "I predict that metal objects will feel ... and non-metal objects will feel ..." Fill in the gaps with what you expect (for example, cold, shiny, heavy, or dull).
explorations.answer: Which test did you find most useful for telling metals from non-metals? Why? Was any object hard to classify? Which one, and why was it tricky? The glass jar may have rung when you tapped it. Does this prove it is a metal? Explain. Which objects felt coldest, and what does this tell you about how…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To investigate everyday objects and decide whether each is made of a metal or a non-metal by testing its properties.

explorations.title: Make Your Own Colour-Changing Indicator
explorations.context: To make a natural acid-alkali indicator from red cabbage (or dark hibiscus flowers or beetroot) that changes colour to reveal whether a liquid is an acid or an alkali.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "I think the cabbage juice will change to ... colour in a sour liquid like lemon, and to ... colour in a soapy liquid."
explorations.answer: What colour is your plain indicator before adding anything? Why do we use warm water rather than cold water to get the colour out faster? Which plants worked best for you if you tried more than one?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To make a natural acid-alkali indicator from red cabbage (or dark hibiscus flowers or beetroot) that changes colour to reveal whether a liquid is an acid or an alkali.

explorations.title: Testing Household Liquids for Acid and Alkali
explorations.context: To use your homemade indicator to sort common household liquids into acids, alkalis and neutral substances.
explorations.prompt: Predict which liquids will be acidic (sour ones) and which will be alkaline (soapy ones) before you test. Write your predictions in the table.
explorations.answer: Which liquids turned the indicator red or pink? What does that tell you about them? Which liquid stayed closest to purple, showing it is neutral? Were any of your predictions wrong? Being wrong is normal in science - what did you learn? Sort your liquids into two lists: acids and alkalis.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To use your homemade indicator to sort common household liquids into acids, alkalis and neutral substances.

explorations.title: The Fizzing Reaction of an Acid and an Alkali
explorations.context: To watch what happens when an acid (vinegar) meets an alkali (baking soda), and to link the fizzing to the idea of neutralisation.
explorations.prompt: Predict: "When I add baking soda to vinegar I think I will see ..."
explorations.answer: What caused the fizzing? (It is a gas being made in the reaction.) If you used the indicator, how did the colour change, and what does that tell you about the pH moving? The vinegar is an acid and the baking soda is an alkali. Use the word neutralisation to explain what happened. Would the reaction…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To watch what happens when an acid (vinegar) meets an alkali (baking soda), and to link the fizzing to the idea of neutralisation.

methods.title: The Metal and Non-metal Property Hunt
methods.example: To investigate everyday objects and decide whether each is made of a metal or a non-metal by testing its properties.
steps: Lay all the objects on a table.
Look: Is each object shiny or dull? Write it down.
Listen: Gently tap each object with the metal spoon. Does it ring like a bell (sonorous) or make a dull thud?
Feel: Touch each object. Does it feel cold to the touch (metals carry heat away from your hand, so they feel cold)?
Bend test (gently): Without breaking anything, decide which objects seem strong and which seem brittle. Do NOT force or snap them.

methods.title: Make Your Own Colour-Changing Indicator
methods.example: To make a natural acid-alkali indicator from red cabbage (or dark hibiscus flowers or beetroot) that changes colour to reveal whether a liquid is an acid or an alkali.
steps: Ask an adult to help you tear the red cabbage leaves into small pieces and put them in a bowl.
Pour warm (not boiling) water over the pieces until they are covered. Let them soak for about 10 minutes, pressing gently, until the water turns deep purple.
Strain the purple liquid through a sieve or clean cloth into a clean cup. This purple liquid is your indicator.
Pour a little of the indicator into three or four clear cups.
Keep one cup as your 'plain' colour to compare against. You will add test liquids in Experiment 3.

methods.title: Testing Household Liquids for Acid and Alkali
methods.example: To use your homemade indicator to sort common household liquids into acids, alkalis and neutral substances.
steps: Put a little indicator into a separate clear cup for each liquid you will test.
Add a small spoonful of the first test liquid to its cup of indicator and stir gently.
Watch the colour change carefully and write down the new colour.
Reddish or pink means an acid; purple means neutral; blue, green or yellow means an alkali.
Repeat for each liquid, using a clean spoon each time so you do not mix them.

methods.title: The Fizzing Reaction of an Acid and an Alkali
methods.example: To watch what happens when an acid (vinegar) meets an alkali (baking soda), and to link the fizzing to the idea of neutralisation.
steps: Pour about two fingers' depth of vinegar into the glass, standing in a bowl.
Optional: add a little cabbage indicator so you can watch the colour as well - it should look reddish in the vinegar (acid).
Add one spoonful of baking soda and stand back. Watch the fizzing bubbles.
Watch what happens to the indicator colour as you add more baking soda a little at a time.
Record what you see, hear and (from a safe distance) smell.

workedExamples.title: Reading the pH Scale
workedExamples.prompt: Reading pH is a skill you can practise. Follow the reasoning in each example.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1 Question: Amina tests some vinegar with universal indicator and it turns orange. Roughly what is the pH, and is the vinegar acidic, neutral or alkaline? Step 1 - Recall the colour code. Universal indicator turns red at the strongest acids (around pH 1) and moves through orange and yellow for weaker acids (about pH 3 to 6). Step 2 - Match the colour. Orange sits between red and yellow, so it points to a weakly acidic liquid, about pH 3 to 4.

workedExamples.prompt: Question: Amina tests some vinegar with universal indicator and it turns orange. Roughly what is the pH, and is the vinegar acidic, neutral or alkaline?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Recall the colour code. Universal indicator turns red at the strongest acids (around pH 1) and moves through orange and yellow for weaker acids (about pH 3 to 6). Step 2 - Match the colour. Orange sits between red and yellow, so it points to a weakly acidic liquid, about pH 3 to 4. Answer: The vinegar has a pH of roughly 3 to 4, so it is acidic - which fits, because vinegar tastes sour. Worked Example 2

workedExamples.prompt: Question: Yusuf tests soapy water and universal indicator turns blue. What is the approximate pH, and how should he describe the liquid?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Recall the code. Green is neutral (pH 7); blue means weakly alkaline (around pH 8 to 10); purple means strongly alkaline (pH 13 to 14). Step 2 - Match. Blue is above green but not as far as purple, so the liquid is weakly alkaline, about pH 9. Answer: The soapy water is about pH 9 and is a weak alkali - which fits its slippery, soapy feel. Worked Example 3

workedExamples.prompt: Question: Khadija slowly adds baking soda (a mild alkali) to some lemon juice (an acid), testing the pH as she goes. It starts at pH 2 and rises to pH 5, then pH 7. What is happening, and what is this process called?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Notice the direction. The pH is rising from a low, acidic number towards 7. Adding an alkali is cancelling out the acid. Step 2 - Name it. Moving an acid up towards pH 7 by adding an alkali is neutralisation. At pH 7 the mixture is neutral. Answer: The alkali is neutralising the acid; when it reaches pH 7 the liquid is neutral. Reading Comprehension - The Rusty Roof of Berbera

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a property of a typical metal? (a) dull (b) brittle (c) conducts electricity (d) a gas at room temperature
workedExamples.solution: (c) conducts electricity - conducting electricity is a key metal property; dull, brittle and being a gas describe non-metals.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which property means 'can be drawn out into wires'? (a) malleable (b) ductile (c) sonorous (d) magnetic
workedExamples.solution: (b) ductile - ductile means drawn into wires; malleable means hammered into shape.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: The only metal that is a liquid at room temperature is: (a) iron (b) copper (c) mercury (d) gold
workedExamples.solution: (c) mercury - it is the one metal that is liquid at room temperature.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a non-metal? (a) sulfur (b) aluminium (c) nickel (d) copper
workedExamples.solution: (a) sulfur - the others (aluminium, nickel, copper) are all metals.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a property of a typical metal? (a) dull (b) brittle (c) conducts electricity (d) a gas at room temperature
practice.answer: (c) conducts electricity - conducting electricity is a key metal property; dull, brittle and being a gas describe non-metals.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which property means 'can be drawn out into wires'? (a) malleable (b) ductile (c) sonorous (d) magnetic
practice.answer: (b) ductile - ductile means drawn into wires; malleable means hammered into shape.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The only metal that is a liquid at room temperature is: (a) iron (b) copper (c) mercury (d) gold
practice.answer: (c) mercury - it is the one metal that is liquid at room temperature.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a non-metal? (a) sulfur (b) aluminium (c) nickel (d) copper
practice.answer: (a) sulfur - the others (aluminium, nickel, copper) are all metals.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An alloy is: (a) a pure metal (b) a mixture of a metal with other elements (c) a type of acid (d) a non-metal
practice.answer: (b) a mixture of a metal with other elements - that is exactly what an alloy is.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Steel is an alloy of iron and: (a) tin (b) zinc (c) carbon (d) gold
practice.answer: (c) carbon - steel is iron mixed with a small amount of carbon.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To separate a magnetic solid from a non-magnetic one you would use: (a) filtering (b) a magnet (c) evaporating (d) an indicator
practice.answer: (b) a magnet - it pulls out the magnetic solid and leaves the rest.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A liquid with a pH of 2 is: (a) a strong alkali (b) neutral (c) a weak alkali (d) an acid
practice.answer: (d) an acid - any pH below 7 is acidic, and pH 2 is a fairly strong acid.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Litmus turns which colour in an alkali? (a) red (b) blue (c) purple (d) green
practice.answer: (b) blue - litmus is red in acid, blue in alkali, purple when neutral.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Pure water has a pH of: (a) 1 (b) 4 (c) 7 (d) 14
practice.answer: (c) 7 - pure water is neutral at pH 7.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which household liquid is an alkali? (a) lemon juice (b) vinegar (c) soapy water (d) cola
practice.answer: (c) soapy water - soap is mildly alkaline; the others are acidic.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The reaction that changes an acid towards pH 7 by adding an alkali is called: (a) evaporation (b) neutralisation (c) filtering (d) rusting
practice.answer: (b) neutralisation - moving an acid towards pH 7 by adding an alkali.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Metal and Non-metal Property Hunt
activities.materials: A metal spoon, a metal key, and a coin.; A wooden spoon, a plastic cup, a piece of charcoal, and a glass jar.; A fridge magnet.; Your own eyes, ears and hands for testing.
steps: Lay all the objects on a table.
Look: Is each object shiny or dull? Write it down.
Listen: Gently tap each object with the metal spoon. Does it ring like a bell (sonorous) or make a dull thud?
Feel: Touch each object. Does it feel cold to the touch (metals carry heat away from your hand, so they feel cold)?
Bend test (gently): Without breaking anything, decide which objects seem strong and which seem brittle. Do NOT force or snap them.
Safety: Magnets must never go in or near your mouth — swallowing magnets is a medical emergency. Keep them away from babies and young children.

activities.title: Make Your Own Colour-Changing Indicator
activities.materials: A few leaves of red (purple) cabbage - or a handful of dark red hibiscus petals, or a piece of beetroot.; Warm water (an adult helps with the hot water).; A bowl, a sieve or clean cloth, and several clear cups or glasses.
steps: Ask an adult to help you tear the red cabbage leaves into small pieces and put them in a bowl.
Pour warm (not boiling) water over the pieces until they are covered. Let them soak for about 10 minutes, pressing gently, until the water turns deep purple.
Strain the purple liquid through a sieve or clean cloth into a clean cup. This purple liquid is your indicator.
Pour a little of the indicator into three or four clear cups.
Keep one cup as your 'plain' colour to compare against. You will add test liquids in Experiment 3.
Safety: Only test safe kitchen liquids such as lemon juice and vinegar. Never touch or mix cleaning products like bleach, and never taste anything you are testing.

activities.title: Testing Household Liquids for Acid and Alkali
activities.materials: Your red-cabbage or hibiscus indicator from Experiment 2.; Small amounts of: lemon or lime juice, vinegar, water, soapy water, baking soda dissolved in water, and milk.; Clear cups, a spoon, and labels or paper to name each cup.
steps: Put a little indicator into a separate clear cup for each liquid you will test.
Add a small spoonful of the first test liquid to its cup of indicator and stir gently.
Watch the colour change carefully and write down the new colour.
Reddish or pink means an acid; purple means neutral; blue, green or yellow means an alkali.
Repeat for each liquid, using a clean spoon each time so you do not mix them.
Safety: Only test safe kitchen liquids such as lemon juice and vinegar. Never touch or mix cleaning products like bleach, and never taste anything you are testing.

activities.title: The Fizzing Reaction of an Acid and an Alkali
activities.materials: Vinegar (an acid).; Baking soda / bicarbonate of soda (a mild alkali).; A tall glass or a plastic bottle, placed in a bowl or sink to catch the fizz.; A spoon; optional: some of your cabbage indicator.
steps: Pour about two fingers' depth of vinegar into the glass, standing in a bowl.
Optional: add a little cabbage indicator so you can watch the colour as well - it should look reddish in the vinegar (acid).
Add one spoonful of baking soda and stand back. Watch the fizzing bubbles.
Watch what happens to the indicator colour as you add more baking soda a little at a time.
Record what you see, hear and (from a safe distance) smell.
Safety: Only test safe kitchen liquids such as lemon juice and vinegar. Never touch or mix cleaning products like bleach, and never taste anything you are testing.

activities.title: Observe: Acids and Alkalis
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of acids and alkalis around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Indicators and the pH Scale
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of indicators and the ph scale around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: The substance an object is made from.
A feature of a material and how it behaves.
Element that is shiny, strong, conducts heat and electricity; on the left of the Periodic Table.
Element that is often dull, brittle and a poor conductor; on the right of the Periodic Table.
Can be hammered or pressed into shape.
Can be drawn out into wires.
Rings like a bell when struck.
Lets heat or electricity pass through.
Does not let heat or electricity through.
Attracted to a magnet (iron, steel, nickel, cobalt).
A mixture of a metal with other elements (e.g. steel, brass, bronze).

vocabulary.meaning: The substance an object is made from.
vocabulary.example: A property is simply a feature of a material and how it behaves.

vocabulary.meaning: A feature of a material and how it behaves.
vocabulary.example: A property is simply a feature of a material and how it behaves.

vocabulary.meaning: Element that is shiny, strong, conducts heat and electricity; on the left of the Periodic Table.
vocabulary.example: Define an alloy and use simple particle ideas to explain why an alloy is harder than the pure metal it comes from.

vocabulary.meaning: Element that is often dull, brittle and a poor conductor; on the right of the Periodic Table.
vocabulary.example: In many ways non-metals are the mirror-image of metals: where a metal is shiny, a non-metal is dull; where a metal bends, a non-metal snaps.

vocabulary.meaning: Can be hammered or pressed into shape.
vocabulary.example: Malleable means a metal can be hammered or pressed into a new shape without breaking - think of a blacksmith beating a red-hot bar of iron into the curved blade of a farming hoe.

vocabulary.meaning: Can be drawn out into wires.
vocabulary.example: Ductile means a metal can be drawn out into thin wires - think of copper being stretched into the wire that carries electricity to a light bulb.

vocabulary.meaning: Rings like a bell when struck.
vocabulary.example: Strike a metal and it rings like a bell - this musical property is called being sonorous.

vocabulary.meaning: Lets heat or electricity pass through.
vocabulary.example: An element usually found on the right of the Periodic Table; often dull, brittle and a poor conductor.

vocabulary.meaning: Does not let heat or electricity through.

vocabulary.example: Being shiny; conducting heat; being brittle.

vocabulary.meaning: Attracted to a magnet (iron, steel, nickel, cobalt).
vocabulary.example: Metals have high melting points, and some are magnetic

vocabulary.meaning: A mixture of a metal with other elements (e.g. steel, brass, bronze).
vocabulary.example: Define an alloy and use simple particle ideas to explain why an alloy is harder than the pure metal it comes from.

commonMistakes: Thinking a high pH means a strong acid.
The scale runs the other way.
Its atoms are not chemically bonded.
Glass and plastic can be shiny too.

connections.text: Particles and states of matter (earlier unit) - the same atoms and particles explain melting, mixing and alloys.
Elements, compounds and mixtures - metals and non-metals are elements; alloys and salty water are mixtures.
Chemical reactions (next unit) - neutralisation is a chemical reaction where new substances form.
metals conduct because their electrons are free to move.
Geography / daily life - rusting on the coast, salt from sea water, and safe drinking water all use these ideas.
Find out which metals are recycled in your area and why recycling metal saves energy - a way of caring for the earth as khalifah.

fluency.prompt: Which of these is a property of a typical metal? (a) dull (b) brittle (c) conducts electricity (d) a gas at room temperature
fluency.answer: (c) conducts electricity - conducting electricity is a key metal property; dull, brittle and being a gas describe non-metals.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.answer: (b) a magnet - it pulls out the magnetic solid and leaves the rest.

fluency.errorFeedback: (b) a magnet - it pulls out the magnetic solid and leaves the rest.

fluency.prompt: A liquid with a pH of 2 is: (a) a strong alkali (b) neutral (c) a weak alkali (d) an acid
fluency.answer: (d) an acid - any pH below 7 is acidic, and pH 2 is a fairly strong acid.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (d) an acid - any pH below 7 is acidic, and pH 2 is a fairly strong acid.

fluency.prompt: Litmus turns which colour in an alkali? (a) red (b) blue (c) purple (d) green
fluency.answer: (b) blue - litmus is red in acid, blue in alkali, purple when neutral.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) blue - litmus is red in acid, blue in alkali, purple when neutral.

fluency.prompt: Pure water has a pH of: (a) 1 (b) 4 (c) 7 (d) 14

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) 7 - pure water is neutral at pH 7.

fluency.prompt: Which household liquid is an alkali? (a) lemon juice (b) vinegar (c) soapy water (d) cola
fluency.answer: (c) soapy water - soap is mildly alkaline; the others are acidic.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (c) soapy water - soap is mildly alkaline; the others are acidic.

fluency.prompt: The reaction that changes an acid towards pH 7 by adding an alkali is called: (a) evaporation (b) neutralisation (c) filtering (d) rusting
fluency.answer: (b) neutralisation - moving an acid towards pH 7 by adding an alkali.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: (b) neutralisation - moving an acid towards pH 7 by adding an alkali.

realProblems.prompt: On the coast at Mombasa, iron roofing sheets rust quickly. Explain why the damp, salty sea air makes iron rust, and suggest one way to protect the roof.
realProblems.answer: 1. Iron reacts with the oxygen and water (moisture) in the air to form rust; the salt in the sea air speeds this reaction up, so coastal iron rusts faster. Protect it by painting it (the paint keeps air and water away) or by using stainless steel, which does not rust.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. Iron reacts with the oxygen and water (moisture) in the air to form rust; the salt in the sea air speeds this reaction up, so coastal iron rusts faster. Protect it by painting it (the paint keeps air and water away) or by using stainless steel, which does not rust.

realProblems.prompt: A family boils camel milk in a metal pot but wraps the handle in cloth. Explain the science behind both choices using the words conductor and insulator.
realProblems.answer: 2. The pot is metal because metal is a good conductor of heat, so the fire's heat passes quickly into the milk. The handle is wrapped in cloth because cloth is an insulator - it does not conduct heat well, so the handle stays cool enough to hold.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. The pot is metal because metal is a good conductor of heat, so the fire's heat passes quickly into the milk. The handle is wrapped in cloth because cloth is an insulator - it does not conduct heat well, so the handle stays cool enough to hold.

realProblems.prompt: Sea salt is made along the Indian Ocean coast by letting sea water dry in the sun. Name this separating method and explain why it works.
realProblems.answer: 3. This is evaporation. The sun warms the shallow sea water so the water turns to a gas and escapes into the air, leaving the salt behind because salt does not evaporate.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. This is evaporation. The sun warms the shallow sea water so the water turns to a gas and escapes into the air, leaving the salt behind because salt does not evaporate.

realProblems.prompt: Halima has a bee sting that hurts. Explain, using the word neutralisation, why dabbing on a paste of baking soda might help.
realProblems.answer: 4. Bee venom is acidic. Baking soda is a mild alkali, the chemical opposite of an acid. Dabbing it on neutralises some of the acid, moving it towards pH 7, which eases the sting.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. Bee venom is acidic. Baking soda is a mild alkali, the chemical opposite of an acid. Dabbing it on neutralises some of the acid, moving it towards pH 7, which eases the sting.

realProblems.prompt: Omar wants to know if the water from a certain well is acidic. Describe how he could test it at home using a red-cabbage indicator.
realProblems.answer: 5. Omar makes red-cabbage indicator by soaking chopped red cabbage in warm water and straining off the purple liquid. He adds some indicator to a sample of the well water. If it turns reddish/pink the water is acidic; if it stays purple it is neutral; if it turns blue/green it is alkaline.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. Omar makes red-cabbage indicator by soaking chopped red cabbage in warm water and straining off the purple liquid. He adds some indicator to a sample of the well water. If it turns reddish/pink the water is acidic; if it stays purple it is neutral; if it turns blue/green it is alkaline.

realProblems.prompt: A material is dull, brittle, does not conduct electricity, and shatters when dropped. Is it a metal or a non-metal? Give three reasons for your answer.
realProblems.answer: 1. It is a non-metal. Reasons: it is dull (metals are shiny), it is brittle and shatters (metals do not shatter), and it does not conduct electricity (most metals do).
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. It is a non-metal. Reasons: it is dull (metals are shiny), it is brittle and shatters (metals do not shatter), and it does not conduct electricity (most metals do).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List four properties that most metals share.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. Any four of: shiny when polished; strong and do not shatter; malleable; ductile; conduct heat; conduct electricity; sonorous; high melting point; some are magnetic.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What do the words malleable and ductile mean?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. Malleable means it can be hammered or pressed into shape without breaking. Ductile means it can be drawn out into thin wires.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name three non-metals and give one use for each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. Any three, for example: oxygen - breathing/hospitals; carbon - cooking/purifying water; chlorine - killing germs in water; helium - filling balloons; sulfur - hardening rubber; silicon - computer chips.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain, using particles, why an alloy is harder than the pure metal it is made from.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. In a pure metal the atoms are all the same size and lie in neat rows, so layers slide over each other easily, making it soft. In an alloy, different-sized atoms disrupt the neat rows so the layers can no longer slide easily; this makes the alloy harder and stronger.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe how you would separate salt from salty water at home.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. Pour the salty water into a shallow dish and leave it in a warm, sunny place. The water evaporates (turns to a gas and escapes) and the salt is left behind in the dish. This is called evaporation.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one household acid and one household alkali, and say how each one tastes or feels.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. Acid example: lemon or lime juice, or vinegar - it tastes sour. Alkali example: soapy water or baking soda solution - it feels soapy or slippery.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit you will be able to:
I can list and describe the main properties of metals and of non-metals, and give everyday examples of each.
I can explain why a particular material is chosen for a particular job by linking its property to its use.
I can define an alloy and use simple particle ideas to explain why an alloy is harder than the pure metal it comes from.
I can separate a mixture by choosing the right method - filtering, evaporating or using a magnet - based on the properties of the substances inside it.
I can recognise acids and alkalis, name common household examples, and state how to handle them safely.
I can make a natural indicator and use it to test whether a household liquid is acidic, alkaline or neutral.
I can read the pH scale and place a substance on it, and explain in simple terms what neutralisation means.
